stem: A 42-year-old woman is referred for investigation of possible Cushing syndrome. She has a 12-month history of central weight gain, easy bruising, and proximal muscle weakness. She also reports new-onset hypertension and irregular menses. Her GP performed an overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 156/94 mmHg, HR 78/min, BMI 34 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Central adiposity, moon facies, dorsal fat pad, purple striae on abdomen, proximal weakness grade 4/5
data_table.Investigations: 1 mg DST morning cortisol: 80 nmol/L (suppress below 50); fasting glucose 7.8 mmol/L; K+ 3.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?

options.text: Late-night salivary cortisol on two separate occasions
options.rationale: After an equivocal overnight 1 mg DST (cortisol 50-140 nmol/L), confirmation requires at least one additional screening test. Late-night salivary cortisol on two occasions detects loss of diurnal cortisol rhythm and is recommended as a convenient confirmatory step before source localisation. (Source: eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: High-dose 8 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test
options.rationale: The high-dose DST differentiates pituitary from ectopic ACTH sources after Cushing syndrome is biochemically confirmed. It is not appropriate as an initial confirmatory screening test. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Cushing Syndrome)

options.text: Inferior petrosal sinus sampling with CRH stimulation
options.rationale: IPSS is an invasive localisation procedure used after ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome is confirmed biochemically. It should not be performed before confirming the diagnosis with a second screening test. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Cushing Syndrome)

options.text: CT adrenal glands with IV contrast and thin slices
options.rationale: Adrenal imaging is indicated once biochemical hypercortisolism is confirmed, particularly if ACTH is suppressed. Imaging before biochemical confirmation risks detecting incidentalomas and unnecessary intervention. (Source: eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Pituitary MRI with gadolinium and dynamic sequences
options.rationale: Pituitary MRI is indicated when ACTH-dependent Cushing syndrome is confirmed, to identify a pituitary adenoma. Imaging should follow biochemical confirmation rather than precede it. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Cushing Syndrome)

explanation.summary: Cushing syndrome workup follows a stepwise approach: screening with at least two positive tests, determination of ACTH dependence, then source localisation. The recommended screening tests are the overnight 1 mg DST, late-night salivary cortisol, and 24-hour urinary free cortisol. An equivocal DST result warrants confirmation with a second modality before proceeding to localisation imaging. Premature imaging without biochemical confirmation leads to unnecessary invasive procedures.
key_points: At least two abnormal screening tests are required to confirm Cushing syndrome biochemically
Late-night salivary cortisol detects loss of diurnal cortisol variation and is a convenient outpatient test
Source localisation with ACTH level and imaging follows biochemical confirmation
Morning cortisol above 50 nmol/L after 1 mg DST is considered a positive screen
explanation.pearls: A morning cortisol above 50 nmol/L after 1 mg overnight dexamethasone has greater than 95% sensitivity for Cushing syndrome, but specificity improves when confirmed by two elevated late-night salivary cortisol results collected on separate evenings.

stem: A 48-year-old man presents to a hypertension clinic after his GP identified resistant hypertension on three agents. He has no significant past history other than occasional muscle cramps. Medications include amlodipine 10 mg daily, perindopril 10 mg daily, and indapamide 2.5 mg daily. The indapamide was ceased 6 weeks ago and perindopril was switched to verapamil for screening purposes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 162/98 mmHg, HR 72/min
data_table.Investigations: K+ 3.1 mmol/L (3.5-5.0); Na+ 142 mmol/L; creatinine 78 micromol/L; aldosterone 620 pmol/L (reference 100-450); direct renin concentration 3 mU/L (reference 10-50); ARR 207

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence spironolactone 25 mg daily and repeat ARR in 6 weeks
options.rationale: While spironolactone is the definitive medical treatment for primary hyperaldosteronism, initiating treatment before confirmatory testing and lateralisation would be premature. Spironolactone also interferes with subsequent ARR testing. (Source: Endocrine Society Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange a saline suppression test to confirm autonomous aldosterone
options.rationale: A positive screening ARR with elevated aldosterone requires confirmatory testing before proceeding to lateralisation. The saline suppression test (2 L normal saline IV over 4 hours) is the most commonly used confirmatory test in Australia. Failure to suppress aldosterone below 170 pmol/L confirms autonomous secretion. (Source: eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Request bilateral adrenal vein sampling with cosyntropin infusion
options.rationale: Adrenal vein sampling is the gold standard for lateralising aldosterone secretion but should follow confirmatory biochemical testing. Performing it before confirming autonomous secretion risks unnecessary invasive procedures. (Source: Endocrine Society Guidelines)

options.text: Request CT adrenal glands to identify a unilateral adenoma
options.rationale: Adrenal CT is part of the workup but follows biochemical confirmation of autonomous aldosterone secretion. Adrenal incidentalomas are common on CT and do not reliably identify the functional lesion without biochemical lateralisation. (Source: eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Repeat the aldosterone-to-renin ratio after a further 6 weeks
options.rationale: The screening has been performed under appropriate conditions with interfering medications ceased. The ARR is clearly elevated with suppressed renin and elevated aldosterone, making a repeat unnecessary. Confirmatory testing is the appropriate next step. (Source: Endocrine Society Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Primary hyperaldosteronism is the most common cause of secondary hypertension, affecting 5-10% of patients with resistant hypertension. The diagnostic pathway involves screening with the aldosterone-to-renin ratio, confirmatory testing to demonstrate autonomous aldosterone secretion, and lateralisation to guide surgical versus medical management. Interfering medications must be ceased or switched before screening. Saline suppression testing is the most widely used confirmatory test in Australian practice.
key_points: ARR screening requires cessation of aldosterone antagonists for 6 weeks and ACE inhibitors or ARBs for 2 weeks
A positive screen requires confirmatory testing such as saline suppression before lateralisation
Post-saline aldosterone above 170 pmol/L confirms autonomous secretion in most Australian laboratories
Adrenal vein sampling is the gold standard for lateralisation in patients over 35 years
explanation.pearls: Aldosterone above 170 pmol/L after infusion of 2 L normal saline over 4 hours confirms autonomous aldosterone secretion, and adrenal vein sampling rather than CT alone should guide lateralisation in patients over 35 because non-functioning adrenal incidentalomas are present in up to 4% of CT scans.

stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to a psychiatric outpatient clinic for diagnostic clarification. Over the past 4 years she has had three manic episodes lasting 4-8 weeks each, with grandiosity and reduced need for sleep. During these episodes she also experiences auditory hallucinations and persecutory delusions. Between mood episodes, she has periods of euthymia lasting several months. However, her treating team has documented persistent auditory hallucinations and referential delusions for at least 3 weeks during two of these euthymic intervals when no mood symptoms were present.

data_table.Medications: Lithium 900 mg daily (level 0.7 mmol/L), quetiapine 400 mg nocte
data_table.Investigations: TSH 2.1 mU/L; urine drug screen negative; MRI brain normal

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this clinical picture?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features
options.rationale: In bipolar I with psychotic features, psychotic symptoms occur exclusively during mood episodes and resolve with mood stabilisation. This patient has documented psychotic symptoms persisting during euthymic periods independent of mood episodes, which excludes this diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia with co-occurring bipolar spectrum disorder
options.rationale: DSM-5 does not permit dual diagnosis of schizophrenia and bipolar disorder when symptoms overlap. Schizoaffective disorder was specifically created to capture presentations with both prominent mood episodes and psychotic symptoms that persist independent of mood. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder with a bipolar-type presentation
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires both a major mood episode concurrent with Criterion A schizophrenia symptoms AND a period of at least 2 weeks of delusions or hallucinations in the absence of a major mood episode. This patient meets both criteria with manic episodes accompanied by psychotic features plus documented psychotic symptoms during euthymia. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and Related Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and Related Disorders

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with recurrent mood-triggered episodes
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder is limited to episodes lasting 1 day to 1 month and does not account for persistent psychotic symptoms across years or the concurrent manic episodes seen in this patient. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder with superimposed bipolar mood episodes
options.rationale: Delusional disorder involves non-bizarre delusions without other prominent psychotic features such as auditory hallucinations. This patient has both hallucinations and delusions, and the presentation is better captured by schizoaffective disorder. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and Related Disorders)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and Related Disorders

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder occupies the diagnostic boundary between schizophrenia and mood disorders. The critical DSM-5 criterion that distinguishes it from bipolar I with psychotic features is the requirement for at least 2 weeks of delusions or hallucinations in the absence of a major mood episode during the lifetime of the illness. Mood episodes must also be present for a majority of the total illness duration, distinguishing it from schizophrenia with mood symptoms. Longitudinal assessment with collateral history is essential for accurate diagnosis.
key_points: Schizoaffective disorder requires psychotic symptoms persisting for at least 2 weeks independent of any mood episode
In bipolar I with psychotic features, psychosis occurs only during mood episodes and resolves with mood
Mood episodes must be present for a majority of the total duration of the active and residual illness
The bipolar type is specified when the presentation includes manic episodes
explanation.pearls: The 2-week minimum of psychotic symptoms in the absence of mood episodes is the single criterion that separates schizoaffective disorder from bipolar I with psychotic features, and a negative urine drug screen is essential to exclude substance-induced psychosis before making this diagnosis.

sources.label: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia and Related Disorders

stem: A 56-year-old woman on long-term prednisolone 15 mg daily for polymyalgia rheumatica presents to the emergency department with 2 days of vomiting, diarrhoea, and progressive lethargy after a gastroenteritis illness. She has been unable to take her oral prednisolone for the past 48 hours. She appears profoundly unwell.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.8 C, HR 122/min, BP 74/42 mmHg (unresponsive to 1 L crystalloid), RR 22/min, SpO2 97% on room air
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, dry mucous membranes, diffuse abdominal tenderness, hyperpigmented palmar creases
data_table.Investigations: Na+ 128 mmol/L; K+ 5.8 mmol/L; glucose 3.1 mmol/L; creatinine 142 micromol/L (baseline 68); cortisol 42 nmol/L (taken at 0800)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Send a cosyntropin stimulation test then await results to guide dosing
options.rationale: While a cosyntropin (Synacthen) stimulation test confirms adrenal insufficiency, treatment of suspected adrenal crisis must not be delayed for diagnostic testing. Hydrocortisone should be administered immediately on clinical suspicion. Cortisol can be measured before treatment if it does not delay therapy. (Source: eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Commence IV dexamethasone 4 mg and oral fludrocortisone 100 mcg
options.rationale: Dexamethasone lacks mineralocorticoid activity and at this dose provides excessive glucocorticoid effect without addressing the mineralocorticoid deficiency contributing to hypotension. Oral fludrocortisone is inappropriate in a vomiting patient. IV hydrocortisone at stress doses provides both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Administer IV noradrenaline infusion and broad-spectrum antibiotics
options.rationale: While the presentation overlaps with septic shock, the history of chronic steroid use with missed doses and the biochemical profile strongly suggest adrenal crisis. Vasopressors alone will not correct the underlying cortisol deficiency and the shock will remain refractory. (Source: eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat and IV normal saline bolus
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is a medical emergency requiring immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat followed by 50 mg 6-hourly, plus aggressive IV normal saline resuscitation. At stress doses (above 50 mg daily), hydrocortisone provides sufficient mineralocorticoid activity, so separate fludrocortisone is not required acutely. Treatment should not be delayed for confirmatory testing. (Source: eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Resume oral prednisolone 15 mg with IV ondansetron and oral rehydration
options.rationale: The patient is in established adrenal crisis with shock, requiring IV stress-dose glucocorticoid replacement rather than resumption of the usual maintenance dose. Oral medications are unreliable in a vomiting patient. The maintenance dose of 15 mg is insufficient for the degree of physiological stress. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis occurs when cortisol supply fails to meet physiological demand, commonly triggered by intercurrent illness in patients on chronic corticosteroids who cannot take or absorb their medication. The cardinal features are refractory hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, and hypoglycaemia. IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat is the cornerstone of emergency treatment, providing both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity at high doses. Concurrent IV normal saline addresses volume depletion and hyponatraemia.
key_points: Never delay IV hydrocortisone for confirmatory testing in suspected adrenal crisis
IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat then 50 mg 6-hourly is the standard acute dosing regimen
At doses above 50 mg daily hydrocortisone provides adequate mineralocorticoid activity
All patients on chronic corticosteroids need sick-day management plans for intercurrent illness
explanation.pearls: Patients on prednisolone 7.5 mg or more daily for longer than 3 weeks should be assumed to have hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis suppression and require stress-dose hydrocortisone (100 mg IV stat then 50 mg 6-hourly) during acute illness or surgery.

stem: A 24-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder stabilised on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for 2 years presents for a routine psychiatry review. She mentions she has recently entered a new relationship and is not currently using contraception. She has had no manic or depressive episodes since starting valproate. She asks about the risks if she were to become pregnant.

data_table.Medications: Sodium valproate 1000 mg daily, quetiapine 50 mg nocte PRN
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 72 mg/L (therapeutic 50-100); LFTs normal; FBC normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management regarding her valproate?

options.text: Continue valproate and add high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily now
options.rationale: While folic acid supplementation reduces neural tube defect risk, it does not adequately mitigate the full spectrum of valproate teratogenicity including cognitive impairment (IQ reduction of 7-10 points), autism spectrum disorder, and craniofacial abnormalities. Continuing valproate in a woman of reproductive age not using effective contraception is not recommended. (Source: RANZCOG)

options.text: Reduce valproate to 500 mg daily to minimise the foetal risk
options.rationale: Teratogenic risk from valproate is dose-dependent but not eliminated at lower doses. TGA and RANZCOG recommend avoiding valproate entirely in women of reproductive age unless no alternative is effective and a pregnancy prevention program is in place. Dose reduction alone is insufficient. (Source: TGA)

options.text: Cease valproate immediately and commence lamotrigine at 200 mg
options.rationale: Lamotrigine is an appropriate alternative mood stabiliser with a much lower teratogenic risk, but it requires slow titration starting at 25 mg daily over 6-8 weeks to avoid Stevens-Johnson syndrome. Abrupt cessation of valproate also risks rebound mania. A planned cross-taper is required. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Maintain valproate only if she agrees to use depot contraception
options.rationale: The TGA pregnancy prevention program requires effective contraception while on valproate, but the recommended approach for women planning pregnancy is to transition to an alternative mood stabiliser well in advance. Relying on contraception alone defers rather than addresses the teratogenic risk. (Source: TGA)

options.text: Plan a gradual cross-taper to lamotrigine with effective contraception
options.rationale: The recommended approach is to establish effective contraception immediately, then initiate a gradual cross-taper from valproate to lamotrigine (starting at 25 mg daily, titrating over 6-8 weeks) while ensuring mood stability throughout the transition. This addresses both the teratogenic risk and the risk of mood relapse. (Source: RANZCOG)

explanation.summary: Valproate is classified as TGA Category D and carries the highest teratogenic risk of any commonly used mood stabiliser. Foetal valproate syndrome includes neural tube defects (1-2%), reduced IQ (mean 7-10 points lower), increased rates of autism spectrum disorder, and craniofacial abnormalities. The TGA Pregnancy Prevention Program mandates that women of reproductive age should not be prescribed valproate unless no suitable alternative exists and effective contraception is in place. Lamotrigine is the preferred alternative for bipolar disorder in women of reproductive potential due to its comparatively low teratogenic risk.
key_points: Valproate carries dose-dependent teratogenic risk including neural tube defects and cognitive impairment in offspring
TGA and RANZCOG recommend avoiding valproate in women of reproductive age unless no alternative is effective
Lamotrigine requires slow titration from 25 mg daily over 6-8 weeks to reduce SJS risk
Abrupt valproate cessation risks rebound mania requiring a planned gradual cross-taper
explanation.pearls: Valproate exposure in utero reduces offspring IQ by a mean of 7-10 points compared to other antiepileptics, and this cognitive effect persists regardless of folate supplementation, which is why the TGA mandates a pregnancy prevention program for all women of reproductive age prescribed valproate.

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 5-day history of left-sided optic neuritis confirmed on ophthalmology review. She has no prior neurological symptoms. MRI brain shows the symptomatic left optic nerve lesion plus three periventricular white matter lesions and one juxtacortical lesion, all enhancing with gadolinium. MRI spinal cord shows a single non-enhancing thoracic cord lesion. Lumbar puncture reveals oligoclonal bands present in CSF but absent in serum.

data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: left optic nerve enhancement, 3 periventricular lesions (enhancing), 1 juxtacortical lesion (enhancing); MRI spine: 1 thoracic cord lesion (non-enhancing); CSF: oligoclonal bands positive; serum oligoclonal bands negative; VEP: delayed left P100

lead_in: What is the most appropriate diagnostic conclusion and next step?

options.text: Diagnosis of MS is met and referral for disease-modifying therapy
options.rationale: The 2017 McDonald criteria allow MS diagnosis at first clinical attack when MRI demonstrates dissemination in space (lesions in 2 or more of periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, or spinal cord regions) and dissemination in time (simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, or CSF oligoclonal bands substituting for DIT). This patient meets both criteria. Early DMT initiation is recommended. (Source: MS Australia)

options.text: Clinically isolated syndrome requiring a second clinical event
options.rationale: Under the 2017 McDonald criteria, a second clinical event is no longer required if MRI and CSF findings demonstrate dissemination in space and time. This patient already meets full diagnostic criteria for MS without needing to await a second relapse. (Source: McDonald Criteria 2017)

options.text: Probable MS requiring repeat MRI in 3 months for new lesions
options.rationale: Follow-up MRI to demonstrate new lesions is one method to show dissemination in time, but CSF oligoclonal bands can substitute for this criterion under the 2017 McDonald criteria. This patient has positive CSF OCBs, making a repeat MRI for diagnostic purposes unnecessary. (Source: McDonald Criteria 2017)

options.text: Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder requiring aquaporin-4 testing
options.rationale: NMOSD can present with optic neuritis but typically causes severe bilateral or longitudinally extensive optic neuritis and longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis spanning 3 or more vertebral segments. This patient's MRI pattern with multiple periventricular and juxtacortical lesions is typical of MS. (Source: MS Australia)

options.text: Isolated optic neuritis with incidental white matter changes
options.rationale: Multiple periventricular and juxtacortical lesions with gadolinium enhancement in a young woman with optic neuritis represent demyelination consistent with MS, not incidental findings. Incidental white matter changes are typically non-enhancing and fewer in number. (Source: McDonald Criteria 2017)

explanation.summary: The 2017 McDonald criteria streamlined MS diagnosis by allowing CSF oligoclonal bands to substitute for dissemination in time, enabling diagnosis at first clinical presentation. Dissemination in space requires lesions in at least 2 of 4 characteristic locations: periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord. Dissemination in time can be demonstrated by simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on a single MRI, new lesions on follow-up MRI, or the presence of CSF-specific oligoclonal bands. Early diagnosis enables earlier initiation of disease-modifying therapy, which reduces relapse rates and disability progression.
key_points: The 2017 McDonald criteria allow MS diagnosis at first clinical attack with appropriate MRI and CSF findings
CSF oligoclonal bands can substitute for dissemination in time under current criteria
Dissemination in space requires lesions in 2 or more of 4 characteristic CNS regions
Early DMT initiation is associated with better long-term disability outcomes
explanation.pearls: CSF-specific oligoclonal bands (present in CSF but absent in serum) substitute for dissemination in time under the 2017 McDonald criteria, allowing MS diagnosis at first clinical attack without waiting for new MRI lesions or a second relapse.

stem: A 68-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance with a 2-day history of increasing confusion and urinary frequency. His wife reports he has been febrile at home. He has a past history of type 2 diabetes on metformin and benign prostatic hyperplasia.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.2 C, HR 118/min, BP 82/48 mmHg, RR 26/min, SpO2 94% on room air
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6), suprapubic tenderness, no meningism, warm peripheries
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.29, lactate 4.8 mmol/L; BSL 14.2 mmol/L; urinalysis: leucocytes +++, nitrites positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management priority?

options.text: Insert an indwelling catheter and send urine for culture
options.rationale: Catheterisation for specimen collection and potential obstruction relief is important but should not delay empiric antibiotic administration. The urinalysis already confirms a likely urinary source. Antibiotics within 60 minutes of sepsis recognition take priority. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Obtain blood cultures then commence empiric IV antibiotics
options.rationale: In suspected sepsis, the highest-priority intervention is obtaining blood cultures followed immediately by empiric IV antibiotics within 60 minutes of recognition. The ACSQHC Sepsis Clinical Care Standard mandates this as the time-critical intervention with the greatest mortality reduction. (Source: ACSQHC Sepsis Clinical Care Standard)

options.text: Commence IV noradrenaline infusion targeting MAP above 65
options.rationale: Vasopressor support is appropriate for septic shock refractory to adequate fluid resuscitation, but should follow initial IV crystalloid bolus and empiric antibiotics. Starting vasopressors before antibiotics does not address the underlying infection. (Source: ANZICS)

options.text: Request urgent CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast
options.rationale: CT imaging can assist with source control assessment but should not delay empiric antibiotic therapy in a haemodynamically unstable septic patient. The clinical picture already suggests a urinary source. (Source: ACSQHC Sepsis Clinical Care Standard)

options.text: Administer IV insulin infusion targeting BSL below 10
options.rationale: Stress hyperglycaemia in sepsis requires management but is a lower priority than treating the infection source. Insulin therapy can be initiated after haemodynamic stabilisation and antibiotic administration. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Sepsis is a life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. The ACSQHC Sepsis Clinical Care Standard emphasises time-critical interventions within the first hour: blood cultures before antibiotics, empiric IV antibiotics within 60 minutes, lactate measurement, and IV fluid resuscitation for hypotension. Mortality increases by approximately 7-8% for each hour of delay in antibiotic administration. This patient meets sepsis criteria with a likely urinary source, supported by urinalysis findings, suprapubic tenderness, and systemic inflammatory response.
key_points: Blood cultures should be obtained before antibiotics but must not delay antibiotic administration beyond 60 minutes
Initial fluid resuscitation with 20-30 mL/kg IV crystalloid is recommended for sepsis-induced hypotension
Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor for fluid-refractory septic shock targeting MAP of 65 mmHg or above
Lactate clearance guides adequacy of resuscitation and should be repeated at 2-4 hours
explanation.pearls: Serum lactate above 4 mmol/L in the context of suspected infection is associated with in-hospital mortality exceeding 25% and should trigger immediate sepsis bundle activation regardless of blood pressure.

stem: A 52-year-old right-handed accountant presents with an 18-month history of progressive left hand rest tremor, slowness buttoning his shirts, and reduced arm swing when walking. He has no cognitive complaints and continues to work full-time. General examination reveals mild left upper limb cogwheel rigidity and bradykinesia with reduced finger-tapping amplitude. MRI brain is unremarkable. He is diagnosed with early Parkinson disease and asks about treatment options.

data_table.Examination: Asymmetric left upper limb rest tremor (4-6 Hz), cogwheel rigidity left arm, bradykinesia left hand with decrement on repeated finger tapping, mildly reduced left arm swing, normal gait and balance, MMSE 30/30
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain: no structural lesion, no vascular changes; DaTscan: reduced left putaminal uptake

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Levodopa-carbidopa 100/25 mg three times daily as first-line
options.rationale: Levodopa is the most effective symptomatic treatment for Parkinson disease but is associated with earlier development of motor fluctuations and dyskinesias in younger patients with longer disease duration. In patients under 70 with mild symptoms, a dopamine agonist is preferred initially to delay levodopa-related motor complications. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Selegiline 5 mg twice daily as a neuroprotective monotherapy
options.rationale: MAO-B inhibitors such as selegiline provide mild symptomatic benefit and can be used as initial monotherapy in very mild disease, but their efficacy is insufficient for this patient's functional impairment with bradykinesia affecting daily activities. A dopamine agonist provides greater symptomatic benefit. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Pramipexole titrated from 0.125 mg three times daily upward
options.rationale: In patients under 70 with early Parkinson disease and functionally significant symptoms, a dopamine agonist such as pramipexole is recommended as initial therapy to delay the onset of levodopa-related motor complications. Pramipexole is started at 0.125 mg three times daily and titrated gradually to an effective dose. Patients must be counselled about impulse control disorders. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Benzhexol 2 mg three times daily for the prominent tremor
options.rationale: Anticholinergics such as benzhexol can help tremor-predominant Parkinson disease but have significant side effects including cognitive impairment, constipation, and urinary retention, particularly in older patients. They are generally avoided as first-line therapy in favour of dopaminergic agents. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Amantadine 100 mg twice daily as an initial monotherapy agent
options.rationale: Amantadine has a role in managing levodopa-induced dyskinesias in established Parkinson disease but provides only modest symptomatic benefit as initial monotherapy. It is not recommended as first-line treatment when functionally significant symptoms are present. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Initial pharmacotherapy for Parkinson disease depends on the patient's age, symptom severity, and occupational demands. In patients under 70 with mild to moderate symptoms, dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) are preferred as initial therapy to delay levodopa-associated motor fluctuations and dyskinesias that develop after years of use. Levodopa remains the most potent agent and is preferred as initial treatment in patients over 70 or those with significant functional impairment. All patients starting dopamine agonists must be counselled about the risk of impulse control disorders including pathological gambling and hypersexuality.
key_points: Dopamine agonists are preferred initial therapy in patients under 70 to delay levodopa motor complications
Levodopa is preferred as initial therapy in patients over 70 or with severe functional impairment
Impulse control disorders occur in approximately 15-20% of patients on dopamine agonists
Pramipexole requires gradual titration from a low starting dose to reduce nausea and orthostatic hypotension
explanation.pearls: Levodopa-related dyskinesias develop in approximately 40% of patients within 4-6 years of treatment, and the risk is higher in younger-onset patients, which is why dopamine agonists are preferred as initial therapy in those under 70.

stem: A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 12-hour history of severe headache, fever, and progressive confusion. He has no significant past history and takes no regular medications. On examination he is febrile with marked neck stiffness and a positive Kernig sign. CT brain shows no mass lesion or hydrocephalus. Lumbar puncture is performed.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.6 C, HR 108/min, BP 148/82 mmHg, RR 20/min, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6)
data_table.Investigations: CSF: WCC 1840 cells/microL (95% neutrophils); protein 3.2 g/L; glucose 1.1 mmol/L (paired serum glucose 7.8 mmol/L); Gram stain: gram-positive diplococci

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric antimicrobial regimen?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g 12-hourly alone commenced after CSF results
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone alone does not cover Listeria monocytogenes, which must be considered in patients over 50 years of age. Additionally, IV dexamethasone should be commenced before or with the first dose of antibiotics to reduce mortality and neurological sequelae in pneumococcal meningitis. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV vancomycin and IV meropenem with oral dexamethasone daily
options.rationale: This combination provides broad coverage but is not the recommended empiric regimen for community-acquired bacterial meningitis in Australia. Vancomycin is added empirically only when cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus is suspected. Meropenem is reserved for specific situations. Dexamethasone should be given IV, not orally. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV flucloxacillin and IV gentamicin with IV dexamethasone
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin-gentamicin covers staphylococcal and some gram-negative infections but is not appropriate for community-acquired bacterial meningitis. Neither agent provides adequate cover for pneumococcus or Listeria in the meningeal space. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone, IV benzylpenicillin, and IV dexamethasone
options.rationale: The recommended empiric regimen for suspected bacterial meningitis in adults over 50 in Australia is IV ceftriaxone (for pneumococcus and meningococcus) plus IV benzylpenicillin or ampicillin (for Listeria cover). IV dexamethasone 10 mg should be given before or with the first antibiotic dose to reduce mortality in pneumococcal meningitis. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone and IV metronidazole with IV dexamethasone
options.rationale: Metronidazole provides anaerobic cover and is used in brain abscess management but has no role in empiric meningitis treatment. Benzylpenicillin or ampicillin is required for Listeria coverage in patients over 50. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Empiric treatment of community-acquired bacterial meningitis in Australia follows age-based protocols reflecting the likely pathogens. In adults over 50, Listeria monocytogenes joins Streptococcus pneumoniae and Neisseria meningitidis as a key pathogen, necessitating the addition of benzylpenicillin or ampicillin to ceftriaxone. IV dexamethasone administered before or with the first antibiotic dose reduces mortality and hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis. Gram-positive diplococci on CSF Gram stain strongly suggest pneumococcus.
key_points: Adults over 50 require empiric Listeria cover with benzylpenicillin or ampicillin in addition to ceftriaxone
IV dexamethasone 10 mg must be given before or with the first antibiotic dose for maximal benefit
Gram-positive diplococci on CSF Gram stain are consistent with Streptococcus pneumoniae
CSF glucose less than 40% of paired serum glucose strongly suggests bacterial meningitis
explanation.pearls: IV dexamethasone 10 mg 6-hourly for 4 days started before or simultaneously with the first antibiotic dose reduces mortality by approximately 30% in pneumococcal meningitis, but shows no benefit if started after antibiotics have been given.

stem: A 35-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after being found unresponsive at home with empty amitriptyline blister packs nearby. His partner estimates he may have taken up to 3 g. He arrived 90 minutes after the estimated time of ingestion. He is obtunded with a GCS of 8 and has had one witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure in the ambulance.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128/min, BP 88/54 mmHg, RR 14/min, T 37.4 C, pupils 6 mm bilaterally and sluggishly reactive
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia, QRS duration 142 ms, right axis deviation, prominent R wave in aVR greater than 3 mm; VBG: pH 7.28, pCO2 48 mmHg, HCO3 18 mmol/L, lactate 3.6 mmol/L; BSL 6.8 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most important immediate pharmacological intervention?

options.text: IV diazepam 10 mg to prevent further tonic-clonic seizures
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are appropriate for seizure management in TCA overdose, but sodium bicarbonate takes priority as the immediate intervention. Sodium bicarbonate addresses the cardiotoxicity (QRS widening) which is the primary cause of mortality, and alkalinisation also raises the seizure threshold. (Source: eTG: Toxicology)

options.text: IV flumazenil 0.2 mg to reverse any co-ingested benzodiazepine
options.rationale: Flumazenil is absolutely contraindicated in suspected TCA overdose. It lowers the seizure threshold and can precipitate refractory seizures and ventricular arrhythmias by removing the protective anticonvulsant effect of any co-ingested benzodiazepines. (Source: AMH)

options.text: IV amiodarone 300 mg for management of the wide-complex rhythm
options.rationale: Amiodarone and other class Ia/Ic antiarrhythmics are contraindicated in TCA-induced cardiotoxicity because they further inhibit cardiac sodium channels and worsen conduction delays. The wide QRS in TCA overdose is a sodium-channel effect, not a primary arrhythmia. (Source: eTG: Toxicology)

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube for decontamination
options.rationale: Activated charcoal can be considered within 1-2 hours of TCA ingestion in a cooperative patient with a protected airway. In this obtunded patient with seizures and significant cardiotoxicity at 90 minutes post-ingestion, airway protection and sodium bicarbonate take priority over decontamination. (Source: eTG: Toxicology)

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 8.4% bolus of 1-2 mmol/kg body weight
options.rationale: IV sodium bicarbonate is the single most important intervention in TCA overdose with QRS widening above 100 ms. It works by both alkalinisation (increasing protein binding of the TCA and reducing free drug) and sodium loading (overcoming sodium channel blockade). A bolus of 1-2 mmol/kg of 8.4% sodium bicarbonate should be given immediately, with repeat boluses targeting QRS narrowing and pH 7.50-7.55. (Source: eTG: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes toxicity through sodium channel blockade (QRS widening, arrhythmias), anticholinergic effects (tachycardia, mydriasis, urinary retention), and alpha-1 blockade (hypotension). The hallmark ECG finding is QRS prolongation above 100 ms, which predicts seizures and cardiac arrhythmias. IV sodium bicarbonate is the cornerstone of management, addressing cardiotoxicity through alkalinisation and sodium loading. A prominent R wave in aVR greater than 3 mm is an additional marker of significant sodium channel blockade.
key_points: QRS duration above 100 ms in TCA overdose mandates immediate IV sodium bicarbonate
Target arterial pH is 7.50-7.55 with repeat sodium bicarbonate boluses as needed
Flumazenil is absolutely contraindicated due to the risk of precipitating refractory seizures
Class Ia and Ic antiarrhythmics are contraindicated as they worsen sodium channel blockade
explanation.pearls: QRS duration above 100 ms after TCA ingestion predicts seizures, and QRS above 160 ms predicts ventricular arrhythmias; IV sodium bicarbonate 8.4% at 1-2 mmol/kg should be given immediately and repeated every 2-3 minutes until QRS narrows below 100 ms.

stem: A 54-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents to the emergency department with a 24-hour history of rapidly worsening left lower leg pain and swelling following a minor gardening laceration 3 days ago. He reports the pain is severe and seems disproportionate to the visible skin changes. He has been febrile at home.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9 C, HR 112/min, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 22/min
data_table.Examination: Left lower leg erythema extending above the knee, tense oedema, skin dusky in patches, crepitus palpable over the mid-calf, pain on passive stretch of ankle, reduced sensation over the affected area
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.4 x10^9/L (neutrophils 19.8); CRP 312 mg/L; lactate 3.1 mmol/L; creatinine 128 micromol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Arrange emergency surgical exploration and debridement now
options.rationale: The clinical features of pain out of proportion, crepitus, rapidly spreading infection with systemic toxicity, and haemodynamic instability are highly suggestive of necrotising fasciitis. Emergency surgical debridement is the definitive treatment and must not be delayed by imaging or further investigations. Mortality increases significantly with each hour of delay to theatre. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Request urgent CT with IV contrast to delineate tissue planes
options.rationale: While CT may demonstrate fascial thickening, gas tracking, or fluid collections, imaging should not delay surgical exploration when the clinical picture is strongly suggestive of necrotising fasciitis. A negative CT does not exclude the diagnosis, and delays to imaging worsen outcomes. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence IV flucloxacillin 2 g and elevate limb for 24 hours
options.rationale: IV flucloxacillin and limb elevation is appropriate management for cellulitis, but this patient's presentation with crepitus, pain out of proportion, haemodynamic compromise, and rapidly progressive infection indicates necrotising fasciitis requiring surgery rather than antibiotics alone. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Perform bedside needle aspiration of the affected soft tissue
options.rationale: Needle aspiration may yield organisms for culture but does not change the immediate management, which is surgical exploration and debridement. It should not delay definitive surgical treatment. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Necrotising Fasciitis)

options.text: Administer IV clindamycin and arrange MRI within 24 hours
options.rationale: IV clindamycin is part of the empiric antibiotic regimen for necrotising fasciitis but is an adjunct to, not a substitute for, emergency surgical debridement. MRI is more sensitive than CT but still should not delay surgery when the clinical diagnosis is clear. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency with mortality rates of 20-40% even with optimal treatment. The diagnosis is clinical and relies on features including pain disproportionate to visible changes, crepitus indicating gas-forming organisms, rapidly progressive infection crossing tissue planes, and systemic toxicity. Surgical debridement within 12-24 hours of presentation is the single most important determinant of survival. Empiric antibiotics should be commenced alongside surgical planning but must not replace operative management.
key_points: Pain disproportionate to visible skin changes is the most sensitive early clinical sign of necrotising fasciitis
Crepitus on palpation indicates subcutaneous gas and is highly specific though not always present
Imaging should not delay surgical exploration when clinical suspicion is high
Empiric antibiotics for necrotising fasciitis include meropenem plus clindamycin plus vancomycin
explanation.pearls: Pain out of proportion to examination findings in the setting of cellulitis-like presentation has a sensitivity of approximately 70-80% for necrotising fasciitis, and the presence of crepitus, haemodynamic instability, or skin necrosis should trigger immediate surgical consultation without waiting for imaging.

stem: A 62-year-old woman with known seropositive myasthenia gravis on pyridostigmine and prednisolone presents with worsening dysphagia, nasal speech, and difficulty lifting her head off the pillow over the past 3 days. She recently completed a course of azithromycin for community-acquired pneumonia. She is sitting upright in bed and appears anxious with shallow breathing.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 96/min, BP 142/78 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 93% on room air
data_table.Examination: Bilateral ptosis, weak neck flexion (MRC grade 3/5), nasal speech, pooling of secretions, use of accessory muscles of respiration
data_table.Investigations: FVC 0.9 L (predicted 2.8 L - 32% predicted); ABG: pH 7.36, pCO2 52 mmHg, pO2 68 mmHg, HCO3 28 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most critical parameter to guide the decision for intubation?

options.text: Arterial blood gas pCO2 level repeated every 4 hours
options.rationale: While hypercapnia indicates respiratory failure, pCO2 rise is a late finding in myasthenic crisis and may occur only after the patient is already in extremis. FVC decline precedes CO2 retention and provides earlier warning. ABG alone is insufficient for monitoring respiratory trajectory. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Serial forced vital capacity measured every 2-4 hours
options.rationale: Serial FVC is the most important bedside parameter for monitoring respiratory function in myasthenic crisis. Intubation should be considered when FVC falls below 15-20 mL/kg, when there is a decline of more than 30% from baseline, or when negative inspiratory force exceeds minus 20 cmH2O. FVC decline precedes hypercapnia and provides earlier intervention opportunity. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Continuous pulse oximetry with a target SpO2 above 94%
options.rationale: Pulse oximetry detects hypoxaemia but is a late indicator of respiratory failure in neuromuscular weakness. Patients with myasthenic crisis can maintain adequate oxygenation until shortly before respiratory arrest due to progressive hypoventilation. FVC provides earlier warning of declining respiratory reserve. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Peak expiratory flow rate measured twice daily at bedside
options.rationale: PEFR measures airflow obstruction and is most useful in obstructive airway disease such as asthma. In neuromuscular weakness, the primary deficit is restrictive (reduced lung volumes from weak respiratory muscles), making FVC and negative inspiratory force more appropriate markers. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Myasthenia Gravis)

options.text: Repeat chest X-ray every 12 hours to assess for atelectasis
options.rationale: Chest X-ray may show basal atelectasis from hypoventilation but does not quantify respiratory muscle strength or predict the need for intubation. It provides structural rather than functional information and is not useful for serial monitoring of respiratory trajectory in myasthenic crisis. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Myasthenic crisis is a life-threatening exacerbation of myasthenia gravis requiring ICU admission and potential mechanical ventilation. It is commonly triggered by infection, surgery, or medications that impair neuromuscular transmission such as macrolide antibiotics. Serial FVC measurement is the gold standard for monitoring respiratory function, as decline in FVC precedes hypercapnia and hypoxaemia by hours. The thresholds for intubation include FVC below 15-20 mL/kg, negative inspiratory force weaker than minus 20 cmH2O, or a rapid decline in serial measurements.
key_points: FVC below 15-20 mL/kg or declining more than 30% from baseline warrants intubation
Hypercapnia is a late finding and should not be relied upon to guide intubation timing
Macrolide antibiotics including azithromycin can exacerbate myasthenia gravis
IVIG or plasma exchange are the acute immunomodulatory treatments for myasthenic crisis
explanation.pearls: The 20-30-40 rule for myasthenic crisis guides intubation: FVC below 20 mL/kg, negative inspiratory force weaker than minus 30 cmH2O, or maximum expiratory pressure below 40 cmH2O - any one of these thresholds should prompt urgent intubation.

stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to her GP with a 2-day history of dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic discomfort. She is otherwise well with no fever or flank pain. She is not pregnant, has no drug allergies, and has normal renal function. Urine dipstick shows leucocytes 2+ and nitrites positive.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 C, HR 72/min, BP 118/72 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Mild suprapubic tenderness, no costovertebral angle tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: leucocytes 2+, nitrites positive, blood 1+; eGFR 92 mL/min

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic?

options.text: Amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 5 days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is not recommended as empiric therapy for uncomplicated cystitis in Australia due to high rates of E. coli resistance (approximately 40-50%). Trimethoprim or nitrofurantoin are preferred first-line options. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Cefalexin 500 mg twice daily for 5 days total
options.rationale: Cefalexin is an acceptable second-line alternative for uncomplicated cystitis but is not the recommended first-line agent in Australian guidelines. It has broader spectrum activity than necessary and contributes to antimicrobial resistance when narrower-spectrum agents are effective. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Trimethoprim 300 mg once daily for 3 days total
options.rationale: Trimethoprim 300 mg once daily for 3 days is a recommended first-line empiric antibiotic for uncomplicated cystitis in non-pregnant women in Australia. It has good E. coli coverage, excellent urinary concentration, and a convenient once-daily dosing regimen. Nitrofurantoin is an equally acceptable first-line alternative. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Norfloxacin 400 mg twice daily for 3 days total
options.rationale: Fluoroquinolones including norfloxacin are no longer recommended as first-line therapy for uncomplicated cystitis in Australia. They are reserved for complicated UTIs or when first-line agents are contraindicated, to preserve antimicrobial effectiveness. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: Nitrofurantoin 50 mg four times daily for 7 days
options.rationale: The correct nitrofurantoin dosing for uncomplicated cystitis is 100 mg four times daily (not 50 mg) for 5 days (not 7 days). This option contains both an incorrect dose and incorrect duration per Australian guidelines. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Uncomplicated cystitis in non-pregnant women is one of the most common infections managed in primary care. Australian Therapeutic Guidelines recommend trimethoprim 300 mg once daily for 3 days or nitrofurantoin 100 mg four times daily for 5 days as first-line empiric therapy. Urine culture is not routinely required for uncomplicated presentations but should be sent if symptoms persist after treatment. Fluoroquinolones and broad-spectrum agents should be avoided as first-line therapy to reduce antimicrobial resistance.
key_points: Trimethoprim 300 mg once daily for 3 days is first-line for uncomplicated cystitis in Australia
Nitrofurantoin 100 mg four times daily for 5 days is an equally acceptable first-line alternative
Amoxicillin has high E. coli resistance rates and is not recommended empirically
Fluoroquinolones are reserved for complicated UTIs or when first-line agents cannot be used
explanation.pearls: Trimethoprim resistance in community E. coli isolates in Australia is approximately 20-25%, which is acceptable for empiric therapy; however, if the patient has used trimethoprim in the preceding 3 months, nitrofurantoin should be used instead due to increased likelihood of resistance.

stem: A 38-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 4 days of progressive ascending weakness following a campylobacter gastroenteritis illness 2 weeks ago. He was initially unsteady on his feet and now cannot stand without assistance. He reports tingling in his hands and feet. He has no facial weakness or swallowing difficulty. Nerve conduction studies show demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 62/min (resting), BP 128/76 mmHg lying and 104/68 mmHg standing, RR 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air
data_table.Examination: MRC grade 3/5 hip flexion bilaterally, 4-/5 knee extension, 4/5 ankle dorsiflexion, absent ankle and knee reflexes, glove-and-stocking sensory loss to mid-shin, no facial weakness, intact swallow
data_table.Investigations: FVC 2.4 L (predicted 4.2 L - 57% predicted); CSF: protein 1.8 g/L, WCC 2 cells/microL, glucose 3.8 mmol/L (paired serum 5.2 mmol/L)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 14 days
options.rationale: Oral corticosteroids have been shown to have no benefit in Guillain-Barre syndrome and may delay recovery. Multiple randomised trials have failed to demonstrate efficacy, and corticosteroids are not recommended. (Source: Cochrane: Corticosteroids for GBS)

options.text: Monitor FVC every 4 hours and treat only if below 15 mL/kg
options.rationale: While serial FVC monitoring is essential, this patient already has significant proximal weakness that limits ambulation. Immunomodulatory treatment with IVIG or plasma exchange should not be withheld until respiratory failure occurs. Treatment is indicated when the patient cannot walk independently, irrespective of FVC. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Administer IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days
options.rationale: IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy is used in conditions such as multiple sclerosis relapses and optic neuritis but has not been shown to be effective in Guillain-Barre syndrome. IVIG or plasma exchange are the evidence-based immunomodulatory treatments. (Source: Cochrane: Corticosteroids for GBS)

options.text: Commence IV immunoglobulin 0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days
options.rationale: IVIG at 0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days (total 2 g/kg) is indicated in Guillain-Barre syndrome when the patient can no longer walk independently, which this patient meets. IVIG and plasma exchange have equivalent efficacy, but IVIG is generally preferred due to easier administration and availability. Treatment should be initiated within 2-4 weeks of symptom onset for maximal benefit. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Arrange urgent plasma exchange for 5 sessions over 10 days
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is an equally effective alternative to IVIG for Guillain-Barre syndrome. However, it requires central venous access, specialised equipment, and is less widely available. IVIG is generally preferred as first-line in Australian practice due to ease of administration. Combining IVIG with plasma exchange offers no additional benefit. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Guillain-Barre syndrome is an acute immune-mediated polyradiculoneuropathy most commonly triggered by preceding Campylobacter jejuni infection. Immunomodulatory treatment with IVIG or plasma exchange is indicated when the patient cannot walk independently, has rapid progression, or has respiratory compromise. Corticosteroids are ineffective. Serial FVC monitoring is essential as respiratory failure occurs in approximately 20-30% of patients. CSF showing albuminocytological dissociation (elevated protein with normal cell count) supports the diagnosis.
key_points: IVIG 0.4 g/kg daily for 5 days or plasma exchange are the two evidence-based treatments
Treatment is indicated when the patient cannot walk independently or has respiratory decline
Corticosteroids are not effective and should not be used in GBS
FVC below 20 mL/kg or declining more than 30% should prompt ICU admission
explanation.pearls: Campylobacter jejuni is the most common preceding infection in GBS, associated with the anti-GM1 antibody and the acute motor axonal neuropathy (AMAN) subtype, which generally has a poorer prognosis than the classic demyelinating form (AIDP).

stem: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department within 2 hours of sudden onset right-sided hemiplegia and expressive dysphasia. His NIHSS score is 14. CT brain shows no haemorrhage and no early ischaemic changes. He has a past history of hypertension and atrial fibrillation but is not on anticoagulation. He weighs 80 kg. The stroke team has been activated and IV alteplase is planned.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 192/108 mmHg (confirmed on repeat), HR 88/min irregularly irregular, RR 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air, BSL 7.2 mmol/L
data_table.Investigations: CT brain: no haemorrhage, no early ischaemic changes; INR 1.0; platelets 218 x10^9/L; creatinine 92 micromol/L

lead_in: What blood pressure management is required before alteplase administration?

options.text: No BP reduction needed as alteplase can be given at this level
options.rationale: Alteplase administration requires blood pressure below 185/110 mmHg to reduce the risk of symptomatic intracranial haemorrhage. This patient's BP of 192/108 exceeds this threshold and must be lowered before thrombolysis can commence. (Source: Stroke Foundation Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Reduce BP to below 140/90 mmHg using IV sodium nitroprusside
options.rationale: Aggressive BP reduction to below 140/90 mmHg before thrombolysis is not recommended as it may reduce cerebral perfusion pressure in the ischaemic penumbra and worsen outcomes. The target is below 185/110 mmHg. Sodium nitroprusside is generally avoided in stroke due to its risk of increasing intracranial pressure. (Source: Stroke Foundation Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Administer oral amlodipine 10 mg and recheck BP in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Oral antihypertensives have unpredictable onset and cannot achieve the rapid, titratable BP control required before thrombolysis. The time to peak effect of oral amlodipine (6-12 hours) is far too long. IV agents are required for rapid and reliable BP control. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Defer alteplase entirely as BP above 180 mmHg is a strict exclusion
options.rationale: Elevated blood pressure above 185/110 is not an absolute exclusion from thrombolysis. It is a modifiable contraindication that can be addressed with IV antihypertensive therapy. If BP can be lowered below 185/110 and maintained, alteplase can proceed safely. (Source: Stroke Foundation Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Lower BP to below 185/110 mmHg using IV glyceryl trinitrate
options.rationale: Blood pressure must be reduced to below 185/110 mmHg before alteplase administration and maintained below 180/105 for the subsequent 24 hours. IV glyceryl trinitrate is commonly used in Australian emergency departments for rapid, titratable BP reduction. IV labetalol and IV hydralazine are alternatives. (Source: Stroke Foundation Clinical Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Intravenous thrombolysis with alteplase for acute ischaemic stroke requires blood pressure to be below 185/110 mmHg to minimise the risk of symptomatic intracranial haemorrhage. Australian Stroke Foundation guidelines recommend IV antihypertensive agents for rapid, titratable BP reduction. After alteplase administration, BP must be maintained below 180/105 mmHg for 24 hours. The therapeutic window for alteplase is within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, so BP management must be efficient to avoid unnecessary delays.
key_points: BP must be below 185/110 mmHg before alteplase administration and below 180/105 for 24 hours after
IV antihypertensives such as GTN, labetalol, or hydralazine provide rapid titratable BP control
Oral antihypertensives are too slow and unpredictable for pre-thrombolysis BP management
Elevated BP is a modifiable factor and should not preclude thrombolysis if corrected promptly
explanation.pearls: The target door-to-needle time for IV alteplase in acute ischaemic stroke is under 60 minutes, and pre-thrombolysis BP reduction to below 185/110 mmHg should be achieved with IV agents within 15 minutes to avoid exceeding the 4.5-hour treatment window.

stem: A 44-year-old man who injects drugs presents with a 3-week history of intermittent fevers, malaise, and a new cardiac murmur. He has no history of valvular heart disease. Three sets of blood cultures drawn from separate venepuncture sites all grow Staphylococcus aureus. Transthoracic echocardiography shows a 14 mm mobile vegetation on the tricuspid valve with moderate tricuspid regurgitation. There is no aortic root abscess.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 C, HR 98/min, BP 118/68 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air
data_table.Examination: Pansystolic murmur at left lower sternal edge increasing with inspiration, no peripheral stigmata of endocarditis, no splinter haemorrhages, no Janeway lesions, no splenomegaly
data_table.Investigations: WCC 16.2 x10^9/L; CRP 148 mg/L; creatinine 82 micromol/L; 3/3 blood cultures: MSSA; TTE: 14 mm tricuspid valve vegetation, moderate TR, no abscess

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial empiric antibiotic regimen while awaiting sensitivities?

options.text: IV flucloxacillin 2 g 6-hourly for native valve MSSA
options.rationale: For native valve endocarditis caused by methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus, IV flucloxacillin 2 g 6-hourly is the recommended first-line agent in Australian guidelines. Treatment duration for right-sided native valve endocarditis is typically 4-6 weeks. The addition of gentamicin is no longer routinely recommended for MSSA native valve endocarditis due to nephrotoxicity without proven mortality benefit. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV vancomycin 25-30 mg/kg loading then trough-guided dosing
options.rationale: IV vancomycin is reserved for MRSA endocarditis or patients with true penicillin allergy. For MSSA infections, flucloxacillin is significantly more effective than vancomycin, with studies showing higher cure rates and lower mortality with beta-lactam therapy. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV benzylpenicillin 1.8 g 4-hourly with IV gentamicin
options.rationale: Benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin is the recommended regimen for native valve endocarditis caused by viridans group streptococci, not staphylococci. Staphylococcus aureus is typically resistant to benzylpenicillin through beta-lactamase production. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 2 g daily with IV gentamicin 1 mg/kg dosing
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone plus gentamicin is an empiric regimen sometimes used before culture results are available or for HACEK group endocarditis. With culture-confirmed MSSA, flucloxacillin is the optimal targeted therapy with superior staphylococcal activity. (Source: eTG: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV daptomycin 8-10 mg/kg daily as a single-agent regimen
options.rationale: Daptomycin is an alternative for MRSA endocarditis or when beta-lactams and vancomycin cannot be used. It should not be used for right-sided endocarditis with pulmonary involvement as it is inactivated by pulmonary surfactant. For MSSA, flucloxacillin remains superior. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Infective endocarditis caused by MSSA on a native valve is best treated with IV flucloxacillin, which provides superior staphylococcal killing compared to vancomycin or other alternatives. This patient meets modified Duke criteria for definite endocarditis with two major criteria: persistently positive blood cultures with a typical organism and echocardiographic evidence of vegetation. Right-sided endocarditis in injection drug users most commonly involves the tricuspid valve and has a better prognosis than left-sided disease.
key_points: IV flucloxacillin is first-line for MSSA native valve endocarditis in Australian guidelines
Vancomycin should only be used for MRSA or true beta-lactam allergy as outcomes are inferior for MSSA
Routine addition of gentamicin for MSSA native valve endocarditis is no longer recommended
Right-sided tricuspid valve endocarditis has a better prognosis with lower surgical intervention rates
explanation.pearls: Daptomycin is inactivated by pulmonary surfactant and must not be used for left-sided endocarditis with septic pulmonary emboli, and its use in right-sided endocarditis with lung involvement remains controversial for the same reason.

stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to her GP requesting preventive therapy for migraine. She has been experiencing 6-8 migraine days per month for the past year despite optimising acute triptan use. She has a past history of hypertension well controlled on perindopril 10 mg daily, and a BMI of 32 kg/m2. She also reports low mood but is not taking antidepressants. She mentions she is keen to avoid weight gain.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 134/82 mmHg, HR 68/min, BMI 32 kg/m2
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 10 mg daily, sumatriptan 50 mg PRN (using 6-8 days per month)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line preventive medication?

options.text: Amitriptyline 10 mg nocte titrated up to 50 mg as tolerated
options.rationale: Amitriptyline is an effective migraine preventive and also addresses comorbid low mood, but it causes significant weight gain which this patient wishes to avoid. It is also associated with sedation and anticholinergic side effects. In a patient requesting weight-neutral options, alternatives are preferred. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Candesartan 8 mg daily titrated up to 16 mg as tolerated
options.rationale: Candesartan is an effective migraine preventive with the dual benefit of treating comorbid hypertension. It is weight-neutral and well tolerated. Given this patient's concurrent hypertension and desire to avoid weight gain, candesartan can replace or complement her current antihypertensive while providing migraine prevention. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Sodium valproate 200 mg twice daily titrated to 500 mg BD
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is an effective migraine preventive but causes weight gain in a significant proportion of patients and is teratogenic. It is not ideal for this woman of reproductive age who specifically requests weight-neutral treatment. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Propranolol 40 mg twice daily titrated to 80 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Propranolol is a well-established migraine preventive that also has antihypertensive properties. However, beta-blockers can cause weight gain, fatigue, and exercise intolerance. In patients concerned about weight gain, candesartan offers similar dual benefit without the metabolic effects. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Topiramate 25 mg nocte titrated up to 100 mg as tolerated
options.rationale: Topiramate is effective for migraine prevention and promotes weight loss, which might seem attractive. However, it has significant cognitive side effects including word-finding difficulty, and its teratogenic risk in women of reproductive age limits its use. Candesartan is preferred given the comorbid hypertension. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Migraine prevention should be considered when attacks occur on 4 or more days per month despite optimised acute treatment. The choice of preventive agent should be guided by comorbidities, contraindications, and patient preferences. Candesartan and propranolol both address comorbid hypertension, but candesartan is weight-neutral while propranolol may cause weight gain. Amitriptyline addresses comorbid mood symptoms but causes weight gain. Topiramate promotes weight loss but has cognitive side effects and teratogenic risk.
key_points: Migraine prevention is indicated when attacks occur 4 or more days per month
Candesartan is an effective weight-neutral preventive with antihypertensive benefit
Comorbidities should guide medication choice in migraine prevention
Medication overuse headache should be considered if acute analgesics are used more than 10-15 days per month
explanation.pearls: Candesartan 16 mg daily has been shown in randomised trials to reduce migraine days by approximately 3-4 per month, comparable to propranolol and topiramate, and is the preferred preventive in patients with comorbid hypertension who want to avoid weight gain.

stem: A 62-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe tearing chest pain radiating to his back. The pain began 3 hours ago and has been constant. He appears diaphoretic and in distress. There is a 22 mmHg systolic blood pressure difference between arms. Bedside echocardiography shows a dilated aortic root with an intimal flap in the ascending aorta and moderate aortic regurgitation.

data_table.Vital signs: Right arm BP 188/102 mmHg, left arm BP 166/94 mmHg, HR 108/min, RR 22/min, SpO2 95% on room air
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, early diastolic murmur at left sternal edge, equal femoral pulses, no focal neurological deficit
data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus tachycardia, LVH pattern, no acute ischaemic changes; CXR: widened mediastinum; troponin: 14 ng/L (normal below 26)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence IV sodium nitroprusside infusion for rapid BP reduction
options.rationale: Sodium nitroprusside reduces afterload but causes reflex tachycardia, which increases aortic wall shear stress and promotes propagation of the dissection. It should only be used after adequate heart rate control with a beta-blocker has been established, if additional BP lowering is needed. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ)

options.text: Arrange urgent CT aortogram and defer treatment until confirmed
options.rationale: While CT aortogram is the diagnostic standard, bedside echocardiography already demonstrates an intimal flap in the ascending aorta with aortic regurgitation, which is sufficient to initiate medical treatment. Deferring IV beta-blocker therapy for imaging risks aortic rupture or propagation. Treatment and imaging should proceed in parallel. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ)

options.text: Commence IV esmolol infusion targeting heart rate below 60/min
options.rationale: Type A aortic dissection involving the ascending aorta is a surgical emergency. Immediate medical management targets heart rate below 60/min and systolic BP 100-120 mmHg to reduce aortic wall shear stress. IV esmolol or labetalol is first-line because beta-blockade reduces both heart rate and the rate of rise of aortic pressure (dP/dt). Urgent cardiothoracic surgical consultation is required simultaneously. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ)

options.text: Administer IV heparin bolus and prepare for emergency PCI
options.rationale: Anticoagulation is contraindicated in acute aortic dissection as it increases the risk of haemorrhage into the false lumen and pericardium. The presentation could mimic ACS, but the inter-arm BP difference, tearing pain, and intimal flap point to dissection. PCI would be inappropriate and dangerous. (Source: eTG: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Administer IV hydralazine 10 mg bolus for rapid BP lowering
options.rationale: Hydralazine is a pure vasodilator that causes reflex tachycardia, which increases aortic wall shear stress. Like nitroprusside, it must not be used before adequate beta-blockade in aortic dissection. The priority is heart rate control with a beta-blocker. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ)

explanation.summary: Stanford Type A aortic dissection involves the ascending aorta and is a surgical emergency with mortality increasing approximately 1-2% per hour without intervention. Immediate medical management focuses on reducing aortic wall shear stress by controlling heart rate (target below 60/min) and blood pressure (systolic 100-120 mmHg). IV beta-blockers (esmolol or labetalol) are first-line because they reduce both heart rate and the force of ventricular contraction. Vasodilators without prior beta-blockade cause reflex tachycardia and are harmful.
key_points: Type A dissection requires emergency cardiothoracic surgery with concurrent medical stabilisation
IV beta-blocker is first-line to target heart rate below 60/min before adding vasodilators
Vasodilators alone (nitroprusside, hydralazine) cause reflex tachycardia and worsen dissection
Anticoagulation is contraindicated and the presentation must be distinguished from ACS
explanation.pearls: In acute Type A aortic dissection, IV esmolol (onset 60 seconds, half-life 9 minutes) is preferred over IV labetalol in haemodynamically unstable patients because its ultra-short half-life allows rapid titration and cessation if the patient becomes hypotensive.

stem: A 36-year-old man presents to the emergency department at 2 am with excruciating unilateral right periorbital pain that began 45 minutes ago. He is pacing the room and cannot sit still. He reports similar attacks occurring nightly for the past 3 weeks, each lasting 60-90 minutes. He has no history of migraine.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92/min, BP 148/88 mmHg, RR 18/min, T 36.9 C
data_table.Examination: Agitated, pacing, right eye injected with ipsilateral lacrimation and nasal congestion, partial right ptosis, no meningism, neurological examination otherwise normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate acute treatment for this attack?

options.text: Oral sumatriptan 50 mg with oral paracetamol 1 g and rest
options.rationale: Oral sumatriptan has a too-slow onset (30-60 minutes) to be effective for cluster headache attacks which typically last 60-90 minutes. Subcutaneous sumatriptan (onset 10-15 minutes) is the appropriate triptan route. Additionally, paracetamol is ineffective for cluster headache. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: IM morphine 10 mg for immediate severe pain management
options.rationale: Opioids are not effective for cluster headache and should be avoided. They do not abort the attack mechanism and carry risks of dependence, particularly given the recurring nature of cluster headache. Evidence-based acute treatments include high-flow oxygen and subcutaneous triptans. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: IV dexamethasone 8 mg as a bridging transitional therapy
options.rationale: Short-course corticosteroids such as prednisolone can be used as transitional therapy while commencing a preventive agent like verapamil, but they are not the optimal acute abortive treatment for an individual attack. High-flow oxygen and subcutaneous sumatriptan are faster and more effective acutely. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: High-flow oxygen at 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask
options.rationale: High-flow oxygen at 12-15 L/min via a non-rebreather mask for 15-20 minutes is a first-line acute treatment for cluster headache attacks. It aborts attacks within 15 minutes in approximately 70-80% of patients. It can be combined with subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg for refractory attacks. (Source: eTG: Neurology)

options.text: Intranasal lignocaine 4% applied to the ipsilateral nostril
options.rationale: Intranasal lignocaine is a third-line option for cluster headache with limited evidence and inconsistent efficacy. It is not considered first-line acute therapy. High-flow oxygen and subcutaneous sumatriptan are the evidence-based first-line treatments. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Cluster Headache)

explanation.summary: Cluster headache is characterised by severe unilateral periorbital pain with ipsilateral autonomic features including conjunctival injection, lacrimation, nasal congestion, and ptosis. Attacks last 15-180 minutes and occur in clusters lasting weeks to months. First-line acute treatment is high-flow oxygen at 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask, which aborts most attacks within 15 minutes. Subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg is an equally effective alternative. Preventive therapy with verapamil should be commenced for the duration of the cluster period.
key_points: High-flow oxygen 12-15 L/min and subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg are first-line acute treatments
Oral triptans are too slow in onset for cluster headache given the typical attack duration
Opioids and paracetamol are not effective and should not be used
Verapamil is the first-line preventive agent for episodic and chronic cluster headache
explanation.pearls: High-flow oxygen at 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask aborts cluster headache attacks within 15 minutes in 78% of patients, and unlike sumatriptan has no dose limit, making it safe for patients with multiple daily attacks.

stem: A 74-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden painless loss of vision in her right eye that occurred 4 hours ago. Over the past 3 weeks she has experienced new-onset right-sided temporal headaches, jaw claudication, and scalp tenderness when brushing her hair. She also reports generalised malaise and 4 kg weight loss over the same period.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 C, HR 82/min, BP 154/86 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Right eye: visual acuity - counting fingers only, RAPD positive, pale swollen optic disc on fundoscopy; left eye: visual acuity 6/9, normal fundus; tender, thickened right temporal artery with reduced pulsation
data_table.Investigations: ESR 94 mm/hr (normal below 30); CRP 86 mg/L; platelets 462 x10^9/L; Hb 108 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and urgent temporal artery biopsy
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily is appropriate for GCA without visual symptoms. However, in GCA with acute visual loss, IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy is recommended to maximise the chance of preserving remaining vision and protecting the contralateral eye. (Source: eTG: Rheumatology)

options.text: Urgent temporal artery biopsy before commencing any treatment
options.rationale: Treatment must never be delayed for biopsy in suspected GCA, especially with visual loss. Temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic for up to 14 days after commencing corticosteroids and should be arranged within this window but after initiating treatment. (Source: eTG: Rheumatology)

options.text: IV heparin infusion targeting APTT ratio 1.5-2.5 for the eye
options.rationale: The visual loss in GCA is caused by arteritic anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy from vasculitis of the posterior ciliary arteries, not thromboembolism. Anticoagulation does not address the underlying inflammatory mechanism and is not part of standard GCA management. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: GCA)

options.text: Oral aspirin 300 mg and urgent ophthalmology review within 48 hours
options.rationale: Low-dose aspirin may be considered as adjunctive therapy in GCA to reduce ischaemic events, but it is not the primary treatment. The immediate priority is high-dose IV corticosteroids to prevent further visual loss. Ophthalmology review should occur urgently alongside treatment initiation. (Source: eTG: Rheumatology)

options.text: IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days then oral taper
